androidVersions.title: Android 1.0 – 1.1 (No Codename)
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• First Android OS release
• Google apps (Gmail, Maps, YouTube)
• Android Market introduced
androidVersions.explanation:
Google released its first commercial Android version 1.0 in 2008.
This version was very basic but introduced core Google applications like Gmail, YouTube, Maps, Calendar, and Search.
All these apps were deeply integrated into the operating system, making Android a smart and connected platform from the beginning.
It also supported web browsing using HTML and XHTML, included a camera, Wi-Fi connectivity, and Bluetooth support.
This version laid the foundation for all future Android developments.

androidVersions.title: Android 1.5 Cupcake
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• On-screen keyboard
• Widgets support
• Video recording
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 1.5 Cupcake marked a major step forward in 2009.
It introduced the first on-screen keyboard, allowing users to type directly on touchscreens instead of physical keypads.
This version also added support for widgets, enabling users to place interactive elements like clocks and weather directly on the home screen.
Another important feature was video recording, which allowed users to capture and share videos easily.
Cupcake also started the tradition of naming Android versions after desserts.

androidVersions.title: Android 1.6 Donut
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Improved search functionality
• Support for multiple screen sizes
• Enhanced camera features
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 1.6 Donut improved usability and expanded device compatibility.
It introduced enhanced search features, including voice and text search across apps and the web.
This version also added support for different screen sizes and resolutions, helping Android grow across various devices.
Camera performance was improved, and users could now manage photos more efficiently.
Donut played a key role in making Android more flexible and scalable.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.0 – 2.1 Eclair
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Google Maps Navigation
• Multiple account support
• Improved UI and performance
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Eclair brought significant improvements in performance and user experience.
It introduced turn-by-turn navigation using Google Maps, which was a major innovation at the time.
Users could now add multiple accounts on a single device, improving flexibility.
The UI became more refined with smoother animations and better responsiveness.
Features like Bluetooth 2.1 and pinch-to-zoom made devices more powerful and user-friendly.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.2 Froyo
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Faster performance (JIT compiler)
• Wi-Fi hotspot support
• Improved browser speed
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Froyo focused heavily on performance improvements.
It introduced the JIT compiler, which significantly boosted application speed and responsiveness.
Users could now turn their phones into Wi-Fi hotspots, sharing internet with other devices.
The browser performance was also enhanced for faster web browsing.
Froyo made Android devices much more efficient and powerful.

androidVersions.title: Android 2.3 Gingerbread
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Improved UI (dark theme)
• Better gaming performance
• Enhanced battery management
androidVersions.explanation:
With Gingerbread, Android began to adopt a distinctive visual design.
The interface used a combination of green and black colors, giving it a unique and recognizable look.
This version improved overall system performance and provided better support for gaming.
It also introduced enhanced battery management to increase device efficiency.
Additionally, Gingerbread supported extra-large screens, improved copy and paste functionality, and introduced NFC (Near Field Communication) support.
These improvements made Android more powerful and user-friendly.

androidVersions.title: Android 3.0 – 3.2 Honeycomb
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Tablet-optimized UI
• System bar and action bar
• Improved multitasking
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Honeycomb was specifically designed for tablets, marking Android’s first major step into large-screen devices.
It introduced a completely new UI with a holographic design, different from earlier versions.
The system bar and action bar improved navigation and accessibility across apps.
Multitasking was enhanced, allowing users to switch between apps more smoothly.
This version was mainly used on tablets like the Motorola Xoom and was not available for smartphones.
Honeycomb helped Android adapt to the growing tablet market.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.0 Ice Cream Sandwich
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Unified phone and tablet UI
• Face Unlock
• Improved multitasking
androidVersions.explanation:
Ice Cream Sandwich introduced a unified design for both smartphones and tablets.
It combined the best features of phone and tablet interfaces into a single system.
This version introduced Face Unlock, allowing users to unlock their device using facial recognition.
The UI became smoother with swipe gestures for navigation.
It also introduced the 'Holo' design framework, setting a standard design language for Android apps.
This version marked the beginning of modern Android design.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.1 – 4.3 Jelly Bean
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Project Butter (smooth UI)
• Google Now
• Improved notifications
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Jelly Bean focused on making the user interface faster and smoother.
With Project Butter, animations became more fluid and responsive.
It introduced Google Now, providing smart suggestions and real-time information based on user behavior.
Notifications were improved, allowing users to expand and interact with them directly.
Jelly Bean also enhanced accessibility and fixed many performance issues from previous versions.
This version made Android more polished and user-friendly.

androidVersions.title: Android 4.4 KitKat
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Optimized for low-end devices
• Immersive mode
• Voice search ("OK Google")
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 4.4 KitKat focused on improving performance, especially for low-end devices.
It was optimized to run smoothly even on devices with limited RAM, making Android accessible to more users.
KitKat introduced immersive mode, allowing apps to take full control of the screen for a distraction-free experience.
The 'OK Google' voice command feature was introduced, enabling hands-free search and actions.
It also improved app compatibility, smart caller ID, and overall system efficiency.
This version helped Android expand rapidly across a wider range of devices.

androidVersions.title: Android 5.0 – 5.1 Lollipop
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Material Design
• New animations
• Improved notifications
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Lollipop brought one of the biggest visual changes in Android history.
It introduced Material Design, a modern design system with bold colors, smooth animations, and a clean layout.
The entire UI was redesigned to provide a more consistent and attractive experience.
Notifications were improved and became accessible directly from the lock screen.
Lollipop also enhanced performance and extended 'OK Google' support to work even when the screen was off.
This version set the design foundation that Android still follows today.

androidVersions.title: Android 6.0 Marshmallow
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• App permissions control
• Fingerprint unlock
• Doze battery saving
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Marshmallow focused on improving user control and battery efficiency.
It introduced a new app permissions system, allowing users to control permissions individually.
Fingerprint support was officially added, enabling secure unlocking and payments.
The Doze mode significantly improved battery life by reducing background activity when the device is idle.
It also introduced support for USB-C and improved app standby features.
This version made Android more secure and efficient.

androidVersions.title: Android 7.0 – 7.1 Nougat
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Split-screen multitasking
• Improved notifications
• Better performance
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Nougat introduced powerful multitasking features.
Users could run two apps side-by-side using split-screen mode.
Notifications were enhanced with direct replies and better grouping.
It also introduced Data Saver, file-based encryption, and improved battery performance.
Nougat marked the debut of Google Assistant on Android devices.
This version improved both productivity and performance.

androidVersions.title: Android 8.0 – 8.1 Oreo
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Picture-in-picture mode
• Faster boot time
• Notification dots
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Oreo focused on speed, performance, and smarter notifications.
It introduced picture-in-picture mode, allowing users to watch videos while using other apps.
Boot time was significantly improved, making devices start faster.
Notification dots were added to show app alerts directly on icons.
Oreo also introduced Project Treble, enabling faster Android updates.
Additional features included adaptive icons and Google Play Protect for better security.

androidVersions.title: Android 9 Pie
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Gesture navigation
• AI battery optimization
• Digital Wellbeing
androidVersions.explanation:
Android Pie introduced a more intelligent and modern user experience.
It replaced traditional navigation buttons with gesture-based navigation.
AI-powered battery optimization learned user behavior to extend battery life.
Digital Wellbeing tools were added to help users manage screen time and app usage.
Pie also improved security, privacy, and adaptive brightness features.
This version made Android smarter and more user-focused.

androidVersions.title: Android 10
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Dark mode
• Privacy controls
• Gesture navigation
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 10 marked a major change by removing dessert-based version names.
It introduced system-wide dark mode, improving battery life and reducing eye strain.
Gesture-based navigation was improved with a more fluid and modern experience.
Privacy controls were enhanced, allowing users to manage location and app permissions more precisely.
This version also included many background improvements for performance, security, and usability.
Android 10 made the system more secure and user-focused.

androidVersions.title: Android 11
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Chat bubbles
• Screen recording
• Media controls
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 11 focused heavily on communication and privacy improvements.
It introduced chat bubbles, allowing conversations to float over other apps.
A built-in screen recording feature was added for easy video capture.
Media controls were redesigned for better accessibility and usability.
Privacy improvements included one-time permissions and better control over background access.
This version made Android more interactive and secure.

androidVersions.title: Android 12
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Material You
• Dynamic colors
• Privacy dashboard
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 12 introduced one of the biggest design overhauls in Android history.
With Material You, the UI became highly customizable with dynamic colors based on wallpapers.
The system automatically adapts colors across apps for a consistent look.
A new privacy dashboard allows users to see and manage app permissions easily.
Widgets and animations were redesigned for a smoother experience.
Android 12 focused on personalization and transparency.

androidVersions.title: Android 13
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Per-app language
• Better privacy
• Improved media controls
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 13 continued improving personalization and security.
It introduced per-app language settings, allowing users to set different languages for different apps.
Privacy was enhanced with stricter permission controls and improved security features.
Media controls were redesigned for a better listening experience.
This version also improved performance and support for larger screens and foldable devices.
Android 13 refined the overall user experience.

androidVersions.title: Android 14
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Battery improvements
• Accessibility features
• Larger font scaling
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 14 focused on accessibility, customization, and performance.
It introduced larger font scaling options for better readability.
Battery efficiency was improved with smarter background management.
New accessibility features made the system easier to use for all users.
It also introduced Ultra HDR support and better camera features.
Android 14 enhanced both usability and performance.

androidVersions.title: Android 15
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Multitasking improvements
• Privacy updates
• Satellite connectivity
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 15 focuses on improving multitasking and connectivity.
It enhances split-screen and multi-window functionality for better productivity.
Privacy updates provide more control over user data and permissions.
Satellite connectivity support enables communication in remote areas.
This version also includes system optimizations for smoother performance.
Android 15 aims to make devices more powerful and reliable.

androidVersions.title: Android 16
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Advanced AI Integration
• On-device AI Processing
• Smarter Battery Optimization
• Improved Multitasking UI
• Stronger Privacy & Security
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 16 is expected to focus heavily on AI-powered features.
It introduces on-device AI processing for faster and more private experiences.
Battery optimization will use AI to adapt to user behavior.
Multitasking UI will be improved for better usability on large and foldable screens.
Privacy and security will be further strengthened with advanced controls.
Android 16 aims to deliver a smarter and more efficient system.

androidVersions.title: Android 17
androidVersions.whatsNew:
• Enhanced AI integration
• Better foldable device support
• Improved battery optimization
androidVersions.explanation:
Android 17 is expected to further enhance AI capabilities across the system.
It will improve support for foldable and large-screen devices.
Battery management will become more intelligent and efficient.
The system is expected to deliver a smoother and more adaptive user experience.
Although details are limited, it will focus on performance, usability, and innovation.
Android 17 aims to continue the evolution of smart mobile systems.
